page.uc-high-aov.title: Persistent Cart for High-AOV Shopify Stores — Save Carts
page.uc-high-aov.desc: On high-AOV stores, one lost cart is a big lost order. Persistent Cart keeps a signed-in shopper's cart across devices and browsers, protecting researched buys.
page.uc-high-aov.h1: Why high-AOV Shopify stores can't afford a lost cart
page.uc-high-aov.lede: For high-AOV stores, keeping the cart matters more than almost anywhere else: a single lost cart isn't a few dollars, it's a big-ticket order gone. Expensive purchases get researched across phones, laptops, and days before checkout — and Persistent Cart keeps a signed-in shopper's cart intact across every device and browser in between.
page.uc-high-aov.s1.h: Why does a lost cart cost more on a high-AOV store?
page.uc-high-aov.s1.b:
Because the maths scales with your average order value. On a store selling $30 items, an abandoned cart is a small loss; on a store selling $1,500 items, the same abandoned cart is a major one. High-AOV merchants make fewer, larger sales, so each cart that quietly disappears represents a meaningful share of the day's revenue.

That raises the value of simply not losing carts. Around 70% of carts are abandoned on average (Baymard, September 2025), and while not every recovered cart becomes a sale, the ones that do are worth far more on a high-AOV store. Keeping a researched, high-value cart intact across a shopper's devices is one of the cheapest ways to protect that revenue.
page.uc-high-aov.s2.h: Why do expensive purchases get researched across devices?
page.uc-high-aov.s2.b:
Because people don't impulse-buy a $1,000 order. They compare prices, read reviews, check specs, and think it over — often across several devices and several days. A shopper might start a cart on their phone, reopen it on a work laptop to compare, and return on a home desktop to buy. Older behavioural research points the same way: 90% of people move between devices to complete a task (Google/Ipsos, 2012 — dated, but directionally still true).

The catch is that a default Shopify cart lives in a browser cookie lasting about two weeks, tied to one browser on one device. Switch devices and the cart is gone. It's a reasonable inference, not a measured rate, that for considered high-value purchases these mid-decision device switches are exactly where carts get lost — and where keeping the cart matters most.
page.uc-high-aov.s3.h: How does Persistent Cart protect a high-value cart?
page.uc-high-aov.s3.b:
It saves a signed-in shopper's cart and restores it on any device or browser they log in from. The cart is keyed to the customer ID and your store domain, saved on every add, update, or change, so the order a shopper assembles on one device is waiting for them on the next — same items, same quantities, same notes.

The one requirement is that the shopper is logged in; cross-device sync works for identified customers, not anonymous guests. On a high-AOV store that's a natural fit, because considered purchases usually involve an account anyway — saved details, order history, faster checkout. Encouraging sign-in turns the cross-device cart from a nice-to-have into a reliable safety net for your largest orders.
page.uc-high-aov.s4.h: Is a persistent cart worth it for a high-AOV store?
page.uc-high-aov.s4.b:
For high-AOV stores, the economics are about as favourable as they get. Persistent Cart is a fixed monthly cost priced to your Shopify plan, while a single recovered high-value order can cover many months of it. You don't need a big recovery rate for the maths to work when each saved cart can be worth hundreds or thousands.

It also runs quietly: the default on login is a silent additive merge, so returning shoppers just find their cart waiting — no popup, no email, no friction at the worst possible moment. For a store where each order matters, the goal isn't a clever recovery campaign after the fact; it's never losing the cart in the first place.
page.uc-high-aov.faq.q1.q: Does keeping carts reduce abandonment on high-AOV stores?
page.uc-high-aov.faq.q1.a: It removes one specific, avoidable cause of abandonment: losing the cart when a shopper switches devices. Around 70% of carts are abandoned overall (Baymard, September 2025) for many reasons, and a persistent cart won't fix price or shipping objections. But for considered, high-value purchases researched across devices, keeping the cart intact protects orders that would otherwise vanish silently — which on a high-AOV store is real money.
page.uc-high-aov.faq.q2.q: What counts as a high-AOV store for this?
page.uc-high-aov.faq.q2.a: There's no fixed threshold — it's about the cost of one lost order. If your typical order runs into the hundreds or thousands, gets researched over time, and often involves a logged-in customer, you're exactly the profile that gains most from cart persistence. Furniture, electronics, jewellery, equipment, and B2B parts are common examples, but any store with large, considered orders fits.
